topic: How Lightning Creates Plasma Hotter Than the Sun's Surface
title: Why a lightning bolt briefly becomes a superheated plasma channel

lesson.learn:
Lightning is not just a bright flash. It is a violent electrical discharge that rips through air, turning ordinary gas into plasma for a fraction of a second. That plasma can reach temperatures far above the Sun's surface, which is why lightning can crack concrete, melt sand, and create sharp chemical changes in the atmosphere.

The reason it becomes so hot is that the current moves through a narrow channel, dumping enormous energy into a tiny volume of air. That sudden energy release heats, expands, and ionizes the air faster than it can disperse, so the bolt becomes a brief but extreme laboratory for plasma physics.
lesson.simpler: Lightning is a giant electrical spark. It heats the air so fast that the air turns into plasma and becomes much hotter than the Sun's surface for a moment.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Lightning is powerful because it concentrates a huge amount of energy into a very small channel of air.
lesson.deeperDive:
The bolt starts when electric fields in a storm become strong enough to break down the insulating power of air. Once that channel opens, current surges through it and heats the surrounding gas to tens of thousands of degrees. That heat causes the familiar flash, the thunder, and sometimes a shockwave strong enough to damage objects nearby.

Lightning also changes chemistry. The extreme energy can split nitrogen and oxygen, helping form nitrogen oxides and other compounds. That is why lightning matters not only as a spectacle, but as a real atmospheric process that contributes to weather chemistry and the Earth's nitrogen cycle.
lesson.funFact: A lightning channel can heat faster than air can move away.
references: NASA Earth Observatory - lightning and atmospheric chemistry

people: Benjamin Franklin - connected lightning and electricity
places: Thunderstorm clouds - where charge builds up
Ground strike points - where bolts land
Storm laboratories - where lightning is studied
years: 1900s - lightning becomes a formal research topic
2020s - high-speed imaging reveals more about the channel
concepts: Plasma - ionized gas that conducts electricity
Electrical breakdown - the point where air stops insulating
Atmospheric chemistry - molecules changed by lightning energy
